hadith_defended: Hadith of the City of Knowledge (حديث مدينة العلم)
overview: This volume is dedicated to defending the Hadith of the City of Knowledge ('I am the city of knowledge and Ali is its gate'). The author systematically refutes Shah Abdul Aziz Dehlavi's claim that the hadith is fabricated (Mawdu'). He achieves this by analyzing the chains of transmission, citing major Sunni hadith masters who authenticated it or graded it Hasan (such as Al-Hafiz Al-Ala'i, Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, and Al-Suyuti), and severely critiquing Ibn al-Jawzi for incorrectly including it in his book of fabricated hadiths. The volume also deconstructs and mocks counter-hadiths fabricated by Sunnis to include Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman as the 'roof' or 'walls' of the city.

responses_to_dehlavi.dehlavi_claim_addressed: Hadith of the City of Knowledge: Declares it fabricated and rejected, citing multiple major Sunni Hadith masters.
responses_to_dehlavi.author_response_summary: The author proves that the claim of fabrication originated primarily from Ibn al-Jawzi, whose strictness and errors in 'Al-Mawdu'at' were heavily criticized and refuted by later, more prominent Sunni Hadith masters like Al-Hafiz Al-Ala'i, Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, and Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti. The author provides multiple authentic chains (turuq) from Sunni sources (e.g., Al-Hakim, Al-Tirmidhi, Yahya bin Ma'in) to prove the hadith is at least Hasan (good) or Sahih (authentic).
key_evidence_cited: Statements of Yahya bin Ma'in authenticating the narrator Abu Mu'awiyah

responses_to_dehlavi.dehlavi_claim_addressed: Hadith of the City of Knowledge: Declares it fabricated and rejected, citing multiple major Sunni Hadith masters.
responses_to_dehlavi.author_response_summary: The author exposes the absurdity of Sunni counter-fabrications that attempt to dilute Ali's merit by adding 'Abu Bakr is its foundation, Umar its walls, and Uthman its roof.' He uses Sunni Rijal criticism to prove these additions are the actual fabrications, while the original hadith restricting the gate to Ali is authentic.
key_evidence_cited: Al-Mawdu'at by Ibn al-Jawzi (used against the Sunni additions)

biographical_dossiers.evaluation_summary: Evaluated negatively for his excessive strictness (tashaddud) and carelessness in declaring authentic hadiths as fabricated in his book 'Al-Mawdu'at'. The author shows how later Sunni scholars universally recognized his errors regarding the Hadith of the City of Knowledge.

biographical_dossiers.scholar_name: Ikrimah, the freed slave of Ibn Abbas (عكرمة مولى ابن عباس)
biographical_dossiers.evaluation_summary: Extensively analyzed to show his Kharijite (Ibadi/Sufrite) leanings, his acceptance of gifts from rulers, and the fact that many major scholars (including Malik and Ibn Sirin) rejected his hadiths or called him a liar, undermining Sunni reliance on him in other contexts.
A detailed biography is provided, listing his birth, death, teachers, and extensive works, evaluating him as a strict Hanbali/Taymi scholar whose anti-Shia bias affects his hadith grading.
Evaluated for his practice of Tadlis (concealing defects in the chain) and his theological leanings (Qadariyyah), questioning the absolute reliability of his solitary reports.

argument_lineage_traced.claim: The Hadith of the City of Knowledge is a fabrication (Mawdu').

rebuttal_lineage_cited.what_they_refuted: Refuted Ibn al-Jawzi's claim that the Hadith of the City of Knowledge is fabricated, proving it has valid chains.
Confirmed the hadith has a basis and many chains, refuting the absolute ruling of fabrication.
Systematically dismantled Ibn al-Jawzi's arguments against the hadith, proving it is Hasan (good) and Sahih (authentic) through corroborating chains.
